result:
Midnight in the ruins, moonlight dripping like solder from a cathedral’s absent spire. I walk the nave-less nave, boots crunching on shards of colored glass that once told saints’ stories and now only keep the secret of how color can survive the death of everything else. The air smells of wet oak, incense burned out centuries ago, and the new living scent of moss colonizing stone. Every footstep is an echo teaching itself to a collapsed cosmos, like a child reciting the multiplication table for void. I remember being nine and my mother telling me numbers went on forever—how thrilled and afraid I felt at their tireless whisper behind the curtain of the world. Now, here, numbers still go on, but they have run out of things to count; this is the sanctuary after the final census, the annihilation that lets zero sigh at last.

I kneel amid the fragments, lift a triangle of cobalt still clinging to a lead came: it is thicker than my tongue yet lighter than a promise, and it glints with the faint sideways grin of starlight it imprisoned when the windows were whole. I tuck it inside my pocket next to the other secret—an egg-shaped magnet from the neodymium factory where my brother used to work before factories became obsolete along with brothers. I carried the magnet all this way hoping to feel a tug, but no poles reply; the world’s iron heart has either cooled or fled. We were promised apocalypse, trumpet and fire, but what came was softer—an untwisting, a sigh too long held, like a sweater unraveled by one pulled thread. Apocalypse, I see now, is not an ending imposed from above; it’s an undoing we accrete bead by bead over millennia until the string breaks and we look down at loose pearls rolling between atoms.

The tablet is still warm against my ribs, battery at three percent, reminders flickering like curious fish. DEADLINE TOMORROW, the calendar says, though tomorrow is definition-less here. I thumb open a note I typed on the train while the world outside dissolved into seagulls and static: “Write the unwritable thing: the color of silence and the flavor of departure.” Now I regret the bravado, because departure tastes exactly like this star-cobalt shard dissolving on my tongue—cold, metallic, hint of blood. Silence in turn is echo’s memory: it holds the shape of the thing it replaces. Here, silence is rib-vault shaped, Gothic and stretching; I walk inside its hollow bone.

Someone once claimed ruins are dialogue between human ambition and dumb patience. I disagree; ruins are not debate but translation—the slow conversion of questions into lichens. Yet sometimes, where moonlight strikes right, I swear I see the translation stutter: the former window fizzes a pixel of gold, an afterimage of what the stone used to depict. I angle the flat of the shard like I saw medieval glaziers do, grinding edges so light refracts—a tiny kaleidoscope for whatever survives me: mice? rats? maybe only time’s long arthritic finger. For a moment the alley of collapse becomes the hallway of a childhood home—my grandmother stepping out of the inversion, wearing the amber necklace that broke the day she died. She mouths something, but sound is one of the exiles, so only the shape of her lips matters: Be careful. Or maybe Believe. Or maybe Begin again. The ghost dissolves back into moon-solder.

I remove the magnet, press it against the broken column’s ferrous vein. Nothing, naturally, yet in the act of pressing a pulse ticks inside the magnet itself, the way some meteorites hum under the tongue like faint reminders of their molten birth. Perhaps my body has become coil enough, core enough. I feel a faint torsion within my marrow, as though I am the transformer now. Maybe that’s the new theology: no creator recreates, only rearranges. I am become an impromptu transformer of embers into signal, ruin into origin.

I lie down amid tesserae that once praised eternity. Moon solder floats through the absent roof and coats my skin until I shine fractionally, billions of infinities smaller than any candle needed. The shard in my pocket begins to vibrate—either earthquake or heartbeat, impossible to tell which is metaphor. I picture the egg-shaped magnet traveling in a silent arc back across the continent to the factory floor where molten metals cooled into scallop-edged discs stamped life into refrigerators and MRI machines; but that factory too is bone now, ovens cold, workers’ laughter step-encoded into rust. The magnet remembers the laughter better than the workers themselves; it still carries the imprint of their uniform pockets brushing against its surface. Magnetism is memory you can’t delete, only scramble.

From the choir loft (loft now air, the loft embedded in sky) a single reed begins to play. Not sound—no—but absence of wind shaped like sound, space dented into smokelike melody. Could be a crumpled horn mourning cathedrals, could be just my blood inventing orchestra because it misses audience. The song forms legs, walks down the rib-vault I am lying inside. It pauses above my lips, tries to enter. I open my mouth wider than ribs, give it permission. Inside, notes pollinate the cavities where teeth used to defend certainty. They seed themselves with questions I thought the world would one day answer. What began as funeral is suddenly rehearsal; the silence leaks spice of green.

I rise, slowly, dragging magnet, shard, and the reed-song still expanding inside my throat. Outside, the plaza that once burned heretics is now a meadow of dandelions glowing bioluminescent, petals spelling words too swift to read. One of them detaches, adheres to my eyelash; I blink Morse code: you / are / not / alone. The dandelion explodes into laughter I haven’t heard since my brother tried to blow out electric candles. Somewhere in the meadow a bell tolls without bronze—time itself knuckling a sound it forgot it could make.

I cross the thresholdless threshold. Each step is fresh trackless snow on a surface that refuses to solidify, which is fine; footprints are for retracing, and I have no past left. Ahead the path dwindles to a single word written on air with pollen glow: HERE. I walk into it until it envelops me like a too-large coat. Inside the word I see the underside of words: their hungry hooks, their willingness to bite any throat that pronounces them. I blow gently; the coat becomes wings, thin as paper, strong as answers at the lip of sleep. I ascend, not away but inward, magnet dragging behind like pet comet, shard reflecting every moon solder still dripping off the swallowed spires.

At apex I finally speak the one syllable the magnet hashtagged onto my blood all this way: “Begin.” The word lands—no—becomes—no—installs itself as next brick in a wall that isn’t wall yet. The meadow dwindles to garden, garden to seed, seed to pulse, pulse to hush where even zero stops bragging. From the hush erupts a church newly wrought of everything dampened—rainwater beads, moss threads, cobalt dust, dandelion laughter—all tessellated by the magnet’s longing for form. Inside the church under a sun that has no number yet, the reed becomes throat becomes bell becomes doorway, and in the doorway stands me but younger, holding my grandmother’s amber necklace reknotted by magnetized time. She holds out her palm: in it rests the cobalt shard, its fracture healed like a scar proud and glowing.

I place the magnet inside the shard’s healed seam; the two fuse, a miniature planet with cobalt crust and magnetic core. I spin it on my finger, infant cosmos wobbling like top. From its equator, a ribbon of light unspools: every missed deadline rewound, every unpaid bill hilariously obsolete, the choir loft rebuilt with actual reeds growing out of actual throats. My brother steps through the ribbon, grease on his cheekbones still shining like second skin. We embrace without speaking because words have graduated to the inside of atoms, and now embrace is sufficient grammar. Overhead the sky—still numberless—grins its sideways starlight grin.

Together we walk the restringed nave; our shadows intertwined perform a shadowplay on walls already imagining frescoes: sunflowers grown from bullet casings, lullabies recited by prophets the age of dust. Behind us the magnet-planet orbits just above my shoulder, tugging on the necklace like leash pulling future forward. Every color we lost returns not in overlap but succession, like organ voluntary each key arriving just after its own absence resolves.

When the first breath of day unnameable finally inhales, the cathedral bells—three slats of sunlight hinged on memory—peal. Inside their bronzeless resonance the entire ruined century is forgiven.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
